channel.title: Ajahn Funn Ācāro Quotes
channel.description: Dhamma quotes by Ajahn Funn Ācāro, updated every 24 hours

item.title: Ajahn Funn: This body is also concocted into form and name (nāma-rūpa). In time, it ceases. Where does it go? Earth returns to earth, water…
item.description:
Vipassanā: Knowing the Arising and Ceasing of Saṅkhārā

This body is also concocted into form and name (nāma-rūpa). In time, it ceases. Where does it go? Earth returns to earth, water to water, wind to wind, fire to fire, the space element to space. It all returns. That’s how it is.

Therefore, we are taught to know the aggregate of conditioned phenomena thoroughly. This is the person of wisdom who fully knows the aggregate of conditioned phenomena. Whatever arises, know it completely. Know the conditioned phenomena thoroughly. Know consciousness (viññāṇa) thoroughly — the one that relinks, the one that compounds. These — perception (saññā), mental formations (saṅkhārā), consciousness — know them all. See that they are all impermanent. Whatever is impermanent, that is suffering. Whatever is suffering, that is not self, not a being. So contemplate so that it knows like that. Don’t let it arise. If it does arise, know it in time. That is called vipassanāñāṇa — the knowledge of insight. Insight (vipassanā) for what purpose? To know the arising and ceasing of saṅkhārā, to know the suffering and danger in saṅkhārā, to know the peril in saṅkhārā. This is the arising of insight knowledge: knowledge of arising and ceasing in saṅkhārā. Wherever it arises, we cease it right there. Arise and cease simultaneously.

item.title: Ajahn Funn: This is why we should all contemplate these things, to separate them out and make our choice. We’ve managed to gain birth, but…
item.description:
If We Don’t Take Birth, Where Will Suffering Come From?

This is why we should all contemplate these things, to separate them out and make our choice. We’ve managed to gain birth, but who is it that takes birth? We’ll discover that we’re the ones who take birth. If we don’t take birth, where will these things come from? If we don’t take birth, where will aging come from? When there’s no aging, where will illness come from? When there’s no illness, where will death come from? When there’s no death, where will suffering come from?

Sit in concentration so that it’s constant. At the moment, it’s not yet constant. There’s birth and then there’s aging, then there’s illness. When there’s illness, then there’s dying. When there’s dying, then there’s suffering, spinning around in the cycles of wandering-on (saṁsāra) without ever coming to an end. How many eons? Countless births. For how many states of becoming have you been worried about this and that? You come into this world alone, but then you get worried about what will happen to this person or that. You tie yourself down.

item.title: Ajahn Funn: Sīlena bhogasampadā – "By virtue comes accomplishment of wealth." We have material wealth. Whether in future lives or in this present life, we…
item.description:
Sīlena Bhogasampadā – By Virtue Comes Accomplishment of Wealth

Sīlena bhogasampadā – "By virtue comes accomplishment of wealth." We have material wealth. Whether in future lives or in this present life, we are not a suffering person, not a poor person, because of the power of this sīla. Since we don't do evil, whatever we do goes smoothly, without hindrance or obstruction – we achieve wealth. Sīla brings wealth to us. Sīlena nibbutiṃ yanti – "By virtue one attains Nibbāna." Even Nibbāna is reached depending on this sīla as the foundation. Therefore you should all understand this. This is the practice. The reason we have come here is because we want happiness, we want progress, we want goodness and virtue – nothing else. Whatever we do, we want only happiness, comfort, and freedom from suffering.

Now, let us make merit by practicing tranquillity meditation (samatha bhāvanā). Sit and watch your own merit and wholesomeness. See what the mind is like – look so that you know and see. Don't just do it for the sake of doing it. Make it so that you truly see, clearly know, and realize it for yourself. We want happiness and comfort. Does it come as we want? We listen to see where happiness and comfort are.

item.title: Ajahn Funn: Recite Buddho, Buddho to know the foundation. Wherever that knowing is, establish mindfulness there – that is, at the heart. Focus on Buddho…
item.description:
When the Mind Is Calm and Peaceful – Happiness in the Present and Future

Recite Buddho, Buddho to know the foundation. Wherever that knowing is, establish mindfulness there – that is, at the heart. Focus on Buddho as the one who knows. If the present moment is good, the future will also be good. If the present moment is not good, the future will not be good. Simply put, examine it yourselves.

We want happiness and comfort. As you recite and contemplate, your mind becomes calm and peaceful. It does not run forward or backward, left or right, above or below. It stays fixed right in the middle, at that which knows Buddho. Then the mind is good, peaceful and at ease, cool in heart and mind, without suffering or distress. This brings happiness and prosperity in the present and in the future. So look into it yourselves. You want to be good. Right now it is not good – why is that? Your mind is not good; it is restless and struggling. The mind is not good – it is suffering, chaotic, agitated. This is what leads beings to fall into misery in the present and in the future.

item.title: Ajahn Funn: For example, when you give a gift, you put out the fire of greed. However much greed you have, you give it all…
item.description:
Giving Puts Out the Fire of Greed; Precepts Put Out the Fire of Anger

For example, when you give a gift, you put out the fire of greed. However much greed you have, you give it all up. You observe the precepts to put out the fire of anger. When you feel anger, you have to bring your precepts to mind. Why should you bring your precepts to mind? You think, “Oh. When I’m angry like this, I’ll be reborn ugly, impoverished, deaf, dumb, and crazy.” When you think in this way, you can let go of the anger. Wherever anger arises, it can give rise to killings and stabbings, thefts and robberies. So we should all observe the precepts.

When we observe the precepts, getting our thoughts, words, and deeds in good order—when we don’t create harm, great or small, with our bodies or minds—then none of these troubling things will happen. Wherever we’re reborn, we’ll be people in good order, not harming one another. So try to understand this. This is why the Buddha set out the religion here.

We’re deluded. Moha means delusion. So the Buddha taught us to meditate: Buddho, Dhammo, Saṅgho, then Buddho, Buddho. Be mindful, and you won’t be deluded. When you’re not deluded, you won’t do evil. You’ll be afraid of evil, afraid of doing bad kamma, afraid of the difficulties these things cause.

item.title: Ajahn Funn: Now, we were born – what did we bring along? Some of us are wealthy. Wealthy in what? Some are prosperous, happy, and…
item.description:
Why Are Some Wealthy and Beautiful While Others Suffer?

Now, we were born – what did we bring along? Some of us are wealthy. Wealthy in what? Some are prosperous, happy, and fortunate – not starving, not in want, not suffering, not struggling – beautiful and handsome. Some are starving, in want, suffering, struggling, mean-looking, ugly. Their bodies are deformed and impaired. Why? People are the same: the four elements, the five aggregates, the six sense spheres are equal. So why are they like that? Let's contemplate and see, all of us.

This is it: lacking in action. We haven't cultivated virtue and goodness for many lives and many births, so we are born as people who suffer and are poor. As for cultivating virtue and goodness, the Buddha laid down the Dhamma and explained it clearly. It's nothing far away. We human beings born into this world all have three kinds of aspirations. The first kind: we want material things, possessions, money, and wealth in abundance – we all strive for that nowadays. The second kind: we want beautiful forms, good looks, long life, no disease, no danger. The third kind: we want wisdom, intelligence, cleverness – so we study and learn both inside and outside. But many people cannot achieve their aspirations.

item.title: Ajahn Funn: That is why we have come to remind our minds to meditate, to choose and refine our mind. If we do not choose…
item.description:
Choosing and Refining the Mind: Like Taking the Flesh of a Fish

That is why we have come to remind our minds to meditate, to choose and refine our mind. If we do not choose and refine it, our mind cannot be used. Our mind has both good and bad. We must choose and refine it, just like a fish: it has both benefit and harm. A wise person takes only the flesh – it is easy. A foolish person eats the bones and gets them stuck in the throat and dies. Our mind has everything: petas, hell beings, the deaf, the blind, the mute, the insane, the deranged – all are there. Also good ears, good eyes, beauty – that is there. The mind of a king, a millionaire, a minister – all are there. Deva sons, devas, Sakka, Brahmās – all are there. Choose which one to take. If asked: how can we know the mind of a deva son, deva, Sakka, Brahmā? It is buddho, buddho. Our mind is joyful, our mind is bright, our mind is clear – that leads in this direction. Now the dark ones: our mind is defiled, the mind is not clear, the mind is dark and murky, chaotic, troubled. When suffering mind arises, it leads to an unhappy destiny. The mind that is happy and at ease leads to a happy destiny. This is the practice.

item.title: Ajahn Funn: Thus, the Buddha taught us to have these three duties, to train and cultivate our character. Now, beauty is not the beauty of…
item.description:
Beauty in the Beginning, Middle, and End: Virtue, Concentration, Wisdom

Thus, the Buddha taught us to have these three duties, to train and cultivate our character. Now, beauty is not the beauty of necklaces and garlands. In the Pali, it is said: ādikalyāṇaṃ majjhekalyāṇaṃ pariyosānakalyāṇaṃ – beautiful in the beginning, beautiful in the middle, beautiful in the end. This is true beauty. Beauty in the beginning means having virtue, i.e., restraining body, speech, and mind properly, not committing any fault great or small with body or mind, keeping the mind normal, seeing the normality of our body and mind. When we have normal, proper virtue, then majjhekalyāṇaṃ – beauty in the middle is concentration (samādhi). With concentration, the mind becomes firmly established, the mind is steady and upright, the mind is cool and calm, the mind is happy and at ease. That is samādhi. When it is established, it is steady. People today suffer because they are not steady. Aniccaṃ means unsteady. The Buddha taught us to make the mind steady like Nibbāna, which is permanent, not changing or shifting, free from everything. Because we are unsteady, we are like this. Whatever is unsteady, that is suffering. Whatever is suffering, that is not self. Therefore, he taught us to make the mind steady.

item.title: Ajahn Funn: Buddho means the knower. Pay homage every day. After paying homage, wherever you go, keep it in mind. You must go to the…
item.description:
Examining the Mind Daily: Like Lighting a Lamp in the Darkness

Buddho means the knower. Pay homage every day. After paying homage, wherever you go, keep it in mind. You must go to the temple and observe your mind: focus and see “Buddho, Buddho.” Examine it every single day. If you don’t examine it – missing a day or two – it won’t do. You must examine it constantly. When sitting, examine; when lying down, examine; when standing, examine; when walking, examine. Why examine? So that you know. When you examine, you see: “Ah, our mind is good. Sitting is good, standing is good, walking is good, going anywhere is good – no danger, no enemies, no evil, no harm.” That is good. If we don’t examine, we don’t know. It’s dark like the night – we can’t see ourselves, can we? Others can’t see us; the trees, the ground – we can’t see them; it’s pitch black. But if we examine, it’s like lighting a lamp, or like daytime. We see ourselves, see other people, see all living beings; we see our own life; we see our own suffering and happiness. We were born and we love our life, love ourselves, love what is ours. If that is so, when others harm us – do we feel good? No, we don’t. And others – when we harm them, do they feel good? They don’t either.

Since both sides don’t feel good, we should not harm each other. Likewise, if a thief steals from us, we don’t feel good; if we steal from him, he doesn’t feel good. If he kills us, we don’t feel good; if we kill him, he doesn’t feel good. Since everyone loves their own life like this, then we should not harm one another.

item.title: Ajahn Funn: At this moment, our body is proper, our speech is proper. Only the mind is still wandering; it is not yet proper. See?…
item.description:
The Middle Is Beautiful: Samādhi – The Mind Established Within

At this moment, our body is proper, our speech is proper. Only the mind is still wandering; it is not yet proper. See? What is being proper like? It is not sending out to left or right, front or back, above or below. It stays right in the middle of our chest, right in the middle of our heart. Normal body, normal mind — that we call sīla. That is how it is. Therefore, let us all know, see, and understand. We have come to train and refine our character. We want goodness, for no other reason. This is precisely why. Majjhekalyāṇaṃ — the middle is beautiful, that is one who has samādhi. Samādhi, they say, is nothing else. They say the mind is established. Look: is our mind established or not? What is it like to be established? It is like a post standing firmly like this. As an analogy: if it stands like this, it can be relied upon. If it does not stand, it topples and breaks like this, and cannot be relied upon. Now, our mind does not stay still; it sends itself here and there, to front and back, left and right, above and below — so it cannot be relied upon. We must make it stand, meaning not sending it front, back, left, right, above, below, but letting it stand right in the middle of our chest.
